metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact kind key (or policy — e.g. use a `"__legacy__"` sentinel, or skip the map entry and rely solely on the `signature` mirror) to use when folding a legacy snapshot `signature` record that has no kind into `attestations`.
findings.finding: The fold-in path for legacy snapshots does not specify which kind key the old `state['signature']` record is filed under in `attestations` when no kind is available. An executor must guess (sentinel string, skip entirely, or some default) and different choices produce incompatible behavior.
findings.location: What section — 'Old SNAPSHOT carrying only signature (no attestations) is folded into the map on restore'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the fold-in spec: 'If the old snapshot carries only `signature` with no resolvable kind, insert the record into `attestations` under a sentinel key `"__legacy__"` (or omit from the map and rely on the `signature` mirror only); document which downstream paths consume this sentinel.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement how a retirement/blank-manifest SIGNATURE event updates `attestations`: either (a) when `manifest` is empty and `retired=True`, clear `attestations[kind]` using the PREVIOUS state's kind (readable from `state['signature']['manifest'][0]` before overwrite), or (b) write a tombstone sentinel to `attestations[kind]` so consumers can distinguish 'never signed this kind' from 'signed and then retired'.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how a retirement SIGNATURE event (empty manifest, `retired=True`, as written by `retire_attested_pin`) interacts with the `attestations` map. The plan states blank/unkindable manifests are 'ignored for routing', meaning the retirement event will NOT remove the prior kind-keyed entry from `attestations`. After retirement, `state['signature']` correctly reports unsigned/retired, but `attestations['plan-review']` (or whatever the kind was) still holds the old, now-retired record — creating a stale attestation that consumers iterating `attestations` could misread as still valid.
findings.location: ## What — 'routing by authoritative manifest[0]; blank/unkindable manifest is ignored for routing' / AC: attestations[kind] AND keeps live state["signature"] mirror
findings.suggested_fix: In `process_signature`, before overwriting `state['signature']`, check if the incoming event has an empty manifest (retirement). If so, extract the prior kind from `state['signature']['manifest'][0]` (if present), and either delete `attestations[prior_kind]` or set it to a tombstone. This ensures the map and mirror are consistent after retirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the dual-write live mirror pattern vs a back-compat shim (e.g., `public_state` or consumer-side fallback) that avoids making the flat `state['signature']` permanently dual-written — or confirm Rule-of-Three is met by naming two other established mirror cases in the reducer.
findings.finding: The dual-write 'live mirror' (writing `attestations[kind]` AND maintaining `state['signature']` as a last-SIGNATURE-wins mirror unconditionally) is a novel reducer pattern with no prior art in the codebase — no other multi-key state field maintains a parallel flat mirror — and the plan does not justify this novelty vs the simpler alternative of migrating consumers to read `attestations` directly.
findings.location: ## What — 'process_signature writes BOTH state["attestations"][kind] = record AND maintains state["signature"] as a LIVE mirror'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact map key used when folding an old `signature` record into `attestations` (e.g., a sentinel key like `'legacy'`, or derived from the stored manifest field), confirm the fold-in is skipped if `attestations` already exists (idempotent), and verify compaction replay does not overwrite post-snapshot `attestations` entries.
findings.finding: The fold-in of old snapshots (`signature -> attestations`) is under-specified: old `signature` records have no `kind` field (that is the root problem being fixed), so the plan does not state what map key to use when populating `attestations` from an old snapshot, leaving the fold-in algorithm ambiguous and potentially non-idempotent if replayed.
findings.location: AC line 3 / ## What (Old-snapshot read fold-in paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: In `process_snapshot` fold-in: (a) only run if `attestations` is absent from the snapshot; (b) extract kind from the stored `signature.manifest` field using the same manifest[0] substring rule — if unavailable, use a sentinel like `'__legacy__'`; (c) document that SIGNATURE events replayed after the snapshot will overwrite the sentinel naturally via LWW.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `signature -> attestations` fold-in in `process_snapshot` MUST be guarded by `if 'attestations' not in compiled_state` (exactly mirroring the `managed_refs` guard) so new-format SNAPSHOTs are never re-folded and multi-kind maps are not overwritten on re-read.
findings.finding: The fold-in algorithm (old `signature` -> `attestations` on SNAPSHOT restore) lacks an idempotency guard for the new-format SNAPSHOT case. If both `signature` and `attestations` are already present in a SNAPSHOT's compiled_state (i.e., after a new-format SNAPSHOT has been written), and the fold-in code runs unconditionally, it will overwrite the full kind-keyed `attestations` map with a single-entry map derived from only the flat `signature` — destroying other attestation kinds on every re-read. This is a data-loss path on partial migration.
findings.location: AC: 'Old-snapshot read fold-in: signature -> attestations on restore; post-snapshot SIGNATURE events replay into the map; new SNAPSHOTs carry both.' / src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py process_snapshot()

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback kind key for fold-in when manifest[0] yields no parseable kind (e.g., empty manifest, retired signature, or pre-kind record) — for example 'legacy' — so process_snapshot has a deterministic, unambiguous rule.
findings.finding: The fold-in rule for legacy snapshots is missing a fallback kind key. The plan says 'signature -> attestations on restore' but does not specify which attestations key to use when the legacy signature record has an empty or absent manifest (e.g., manifest:[], a retired signature, or a pre-kind record where manifest[0] has no ':' prefix). process_snapshot must choose a key name, but the plan provides no decision rule for this edge case.
findings.location: AC3 — 'Old-snapshot read fold-in: signature -> attestations on restore'; plan 'What' section fold-in description; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:529 (process_snapshot)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'When manifest[0] is absent or carries no ":" separator, file the record under the key "legacy" (or a named constant ATTESTATION_KIND_LEGACY).' This is deterministic and round-trips correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the live-mirror invariant (most-recent-SIGNATURE event wins globally, per-kind LWW in `attestations`), the kind-routing rule (manifest[0] substring), and the fold-in contract for old snapshots, so future slice authors have a stable reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (live mirror of most-recent attestation + kind-keyed map + old-snapshot fold-in contract) but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc capturing this decision, which is non-trivial and will affect future slices that consume `attestations` directly.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-attestations-kind-keyed-map.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section) documenting: (1) why the live mirror exists and its invariant, (2) the kind extraction rule, (3) the fold-in algorithm for old snapshots, (4) how `public_state()` strips HMAC hex at the kind level.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether a SIGNATURE event with a blank/unkindable manifest (a) updates `state['signature']` mirror only, (b) is fully dropped (no state change), or (c) raises an error — and add this to the AC or routing spec.
findings.finding: The plan states unkindable/blank manifests are 'ignored for routing' but does not clarify whether the `state['signature']` most-recent mirror is still updated for such events. The 'last SIGNATURE event wins' mirror rule and the 'ignored for routing' rule are stated in different sentences with no reconciliation, leaving the executor uncertain about the correct behavior.
findings.location: What section — 'a blank/unkindable manifest is ignored for routing' vs. 'maintains state["signature"] as a LIVE mirror of the MOST-RECENT attestation (last SIGNATURE event wins)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing specification (even a bullet list of test cases) covering: (a) kind routing with match / mismatch / blank manifest[0]; (b) LWW ordering — second SIGNATURE event for same kind overwrites, different kind coexists; (c) old-snapshot fold-in — snapshot with only 'signature' key produces correct attestations map on restore; (d) public_state() HMAC strip applies to every present kind including a novel kind not hardcoded; (e) state['signature'] mirror equals the most-recent SIGNATURE event's record across kind interleaving.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple non-trivial branching behaviors (kind routing from manifest[0] vs data['kind'] hint with validation, LWW ordering across kinds, old-snapshot fold-in, HMAC strip per-kind in public_state, blank/unkindable manifest ignored) but specifies no testing approach, test cases, or coverage expectations anywhere — not in the ACs, not in a testing section, not even a reference to existing test patterns.
findings.location: Entire plan — no testing section; Acceptance Criteria; ## Scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact matching algorithm for the data["kind"] hint check (e.g., strict string equality: `data["kind"] == extracted_kind_from_manifest`), co-located in AC1 or the What section's routing rule.
findings.finding: The `data["kind"]` 'validated hint' matching rule is described in the plan but the matching algorithm is ambiguous. The plan says data["kind"] is 'only a hint, used iff it matches' — but 'matches' is never formally defined. It is unclear whether this means strict string equality with the extracted manifest[0] kind, case-insensitive comparison, prefix matching, or something else. This decision rule is not co-located with the schema or the processor spec.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — kind routing rule; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'hint is used iff `data["kind"] == kind_from_manifest` (exact string equality after extraction); any other value is ignored and manifest[0] is authoritative.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or a note that it is deferred to a subsequent slice) explicitly covering `compute_validity` using `last_reopened_at` to verify 'not reopened since signed_at' — or, if intentionally deferred, document that deferral in Scope.
findings.finding: `compute_validity` is described in the plan body as a consumer of `last_reopened_at` ("so `compute_validity` can check 'not reopened since signed_at' purely from compiled state") but no acceptance criterion verifies that `compute_validity` is updated to use this new field.
findings.location: ## What section: 'so compute_validity can check "not reopened since signed_at" purely from compiled state'
findings.suggested_fix: Either add AC: '`compute_validity` uses `state["last_reopened_at"]` to verify the ticket was not reopened after the most-recent attestation; a ticket reopened post-attestation is treated as invalidated.' — or add a Scope note: 'compute_validity integration is deferred to slice N.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and name the actual location/module of `compute_validity` (or its equivalent) in the codebase, confirm it reads from compiled state, and verify that `last_reopened_at` is the right seam — or clarify that `compute_validity` is to be introduced in a later slice and note which slice owns it.
findings.finding: `compute_validity` is cited as the consumer of `state['last_reopened_at']` but this function does not exist anywhere in the codebase — it is an unverified capability assertion about a non-existent function.
findings.location: ## What — 'so compute_validity can check "not reopened since signed_at" purely from compiled state'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where `compute_validity` lives (file path, module) and its decision rule using `last_reopened_at`, OR explicitly note that `compute_validity` is defined in a later slice and that `last_reopened_at` is added here only as a prerequisite field.
findings.finding: `compute_validity` is referenced in AC2 as the consumer of `last_reopened_at` but does not exist anywhere in the codebase. The processing protocol that would use `last_reopened_at` to check 'not reopened since signed_at' is entirely undefined — neither the function's location, signature, nor logic is specified.
findings.location: AC2 — 'so compute_validity can check "not reopened since signed_at" purely from compiled state'; plan 'What' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exhaustive kind enum/constant set for `attestations` map keys (at minimum: the `plan-review` prefix and the completion-verification manifest[0] prefix), co-located in a constants module or the schema, so process_signature's router and the schema can validate them.
findings.finding: The kind vocabulary for the `attestations` map is referenced but never enumerated. The plan defines kind as 'substring before first ":"' from manifest[0], implying a string-keyed map, but never lists the exhaustive set of valid kind values. From the codebase, 'plan-review' is confirmed (attest.py `_MANIFEST_PREFIX`), but completion-verification manifests also exist and their manifest[0] prefix is never named. An executor implementing the router cannot know whether the completion kind key is 'completion', 'completion-verification', or something else.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — kind routing rule; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:30 (_MANIFEST_PREFIX = 'plan-review'); src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/completion-verification.yaml
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `KNOWN_ATTESTATION_KINDS` frozenset in a constants file (or in `_version.py` alongside `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`), specifying at minimum `{"plan-review", "<completion-kind>"}`. Reference it from both process_signature and ticket_state.schema.json.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering the `ticket_state.schema.json` update — specify that `attestations` (map of kind→record) and `last_reopened_at` (timestamp or null) are present in the schema and that existing snapshots still validate.
findings.finding: `ticket_state.schema.json` is listed in Scope as a required change but no acceptance criterion verifies it is updated, valid, or that the new `attestations`/`last_reopened_at` fields are correctly reflected in the schema.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`ticket_state.schema.json` updated to include `attestations` (additionalProperties of per-kind record shape per 114f) and `last_reopened_at` (ISO timestamp or null); existing snapshots without these keys still pass validation.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out statement: describe what old code (pre-slice) does when it encounters a compacted snapshot carrying `attestations`, confirm `state["signature"]` is always populated so old consumers degrade gracefully, and state whether snapshot repair is needed on rollback.
findings.finding: The plan describes a live-mirror strategy and old-snapshot fold-in for backward compat, but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan if this slice must be reverted after compacted snapshots (carrying both `signature` and `attestations`) have been written to storage.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'Old code ignores unknown `attestations` key and reads `state["signature"]` directly — the mirror guarantee means rollback is safe without snapshot repair, provided the mirror invariant holds. To revert: deploy old code; existing snapshots remain valid. No manual repair needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider extracting `state['last_reopened_at']` (process_status change + AC-2) into its own follow-on slice so that the independent `attestations` map work and the reopen-timestamp work can be reviewed, merged, and rolled back independently.
findings.finding: The plan bundles `state['last_reopened_at']` (driven by `process_status`, serving `compute_validity`) into the same slice as the `attestations` map (driven by `process_signature`). These are structurally independent concerns touching different processors, different consumers, and different ACs — a structural 'and' joining independent goals. If the `attestations` schema or fold-in logic requires revision, the `last_reopened_at` work is blocked or rolled back with it, adding unnecessary blast radius.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (or add a subtitle) to reflect that `state["last_reopened_at"]` is also introduced in this slice, e.g. 'Reducer + state model: attestations{} kind-keyed map + last_reopened_at tracking'.
findings.finding: The plan title ('attestations{} kind-keyed map') under-describes the actual scope: the body also introduces `state['last_reopened_at']` via `process_status`, a separate state field not implied by the title. While the Why section justifies it as needed for `compute_validity`, the title creates a narrower expectation than what is built, which could mislead reviewers and cause scope confusion during implementation.
findings.location: Title vs. 'Also add state["last_reopened_at"]' in the What section; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Reducer + state model: attestations{} kind-keyed map and last_reopened_at tracking' or add a parenthetical — '(includes last_reopened_at for compute_validity)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
